g.desc:
Two isometric views of a wooden chassis. The chassis is a long, rectangular structure made of interlocking panels with curved side supports.
Top Image: Labeled "View with Breadboard on Top," it shows a white breadboard (a grid of tiny holes) mounted flat on the top surface of the wooden frame. At the front end of the chassis, an ultrasonic sensor with two prominent circular "eyes" is visible.
Bottom Image: Labeled "View with Breadboard inside," it shows the same chassis with the top surface appearing as a solid wooden panel, indicating the electronics are tucked into the hollow interior of the frame.
The entire graphic uses white line art on a blue grid background. The completed assembly is approximately 12 inches long, 5 inches wide, and 6 inches tall.
g.title: Completed Assembly Views

g.desc:
An exploded view blueprint showing the initial construction of the wooden chassis. Three main wooden pieces are featured: a long, flat Bottom piece, a small notched Front piece, and a large contoured Left side panel.
First Action: Labeled with orange circles containing the number "1," the Front piece is shown being lowered into two square slots at the front of the Bottom piece.
Second Action: Labeled with orange circles containing the number "2," the Left side panel is attached to the edge of the Bottom piece. Three screws and nuts are shown lining up with three circular holes along the lower edge of the panel.
Detail Inset: A close-up box in the bottom right corner illustrates the fastening method, showing a screw passing through a wooden panel and securing into a hex nut nested in a T-shaped slot.
Text Prompts: "Slide the Front piece into the Bottom piece" and "Attach the Left side with Screws and Nuts."
Tactile Assembly Descriptions:
The T-Slot Fastener: The detail inset is the most important "mechanical rule" for this project. It shows that the nut doesn't just sit on the back of the screw—it slides into a specific T-shaped cutout in the wood, which holds the nut in place while the screw is tightened.
The "Bottom" Piece: This is the largest, flattest piece in the kit, roughly 12 inches long. It acts as the foundation.
The "Side" Panel: This piece has a distinct "boot" or "foot" shape at the front and back, which creates the 6-inch height of the assembly.
Tab-and-Slot: Notice that the Front piece has two large "teeth" (tabs) on the bottom that must align perfectly with the two square "windows" (slots) in the baseplate.
g.title: Step 1 Assembly Diagram

g.desc:
An isometric blueprint showing the addition of the Back piece to the partially assembled chassis. The Back piece is a rectangular wooden panel with a wide, pill-shaped cutout in the center that acts as a handle.
The Connection: Two orange dashed arrows indicate the assembly path. One arrow points to a small notch at the top corner of the side panel. The second arrow points to a keyhole-shaped slot (a circle with a narrow slit extending downward) located at the bottom corner of the side panel.
The Alignment: The Back piece features two small circular tabs (or "pegs") on its sides that must slide into these keyholes.
Text Prompt: "Slide the Back piece into the notch and keyhole."
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "2" is displayed next to five circles, with the second circle filled in to show progress.
Tactile Assembly Descriptions:
The "Keyhole" Trick: This is a "lock-and-slide" joint. A student can feel the round hole at the bottom of the side panel; the peg on the Back piece goes into that round hole and then slides down into the narrow slit to lock it in place.
The Back Handle: The large, horizontal oval hole in the Back piece makes it very easy to distinguish from the Front piece. It doubles as a carry-handle for the finished robot.
Structural Integrity: Unlike the screws used in Step 1, this step relies on the friction and geometry of the wood. Once the Back piece is slid down into the keyholes, it should feel significantly more stable.
g.title: Step 2 Assembly Diagram

g.desc:
An isometric exploded view showing the final major wooden panel being added to the chassis. The Right side panel, which is identical in shape to the left side, is shown being attached to the opposite side of the Bottom piece.
The Connection: Orange dashed lines indicate four attachment points. Three points are along the bottom edge, where screws and hex nuts secure the panel to the base. A fourth point at the rear shows the panel's top notch and bottom keyhole aligning with the tabs on the Back piece.
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "3" is displayed, and three out of five circles are filled in.
Text Prompt: "Attach the Right side with Screws and Nuts."
Tactile Assembly Descriptions:
Symmetry Check: Once this piece is attached, the chassis should be perfectly symmetrical. A student can feel both side panels; they should be the same distance apart at the front and the back.
The "T-Slot" Repeat: Just like in Step 1, the three nuts at the bottom must be tucked into their T-shaped slots before the screws are tightened.
Rigidity: At this stage, the "skeleton" of the robot is complete. The frame should feel very sturdy and no longer wobble when placed on a flat surface.
Keyhole Alignment: Ensure the circular tab on the right side of the Back piece is fully seated in the keyhole of the new side panel before tightening the bottom screws.
g.title: Step 3 Assembly Diagram

g.desc:
An isometric blueprint showing the installation of the Lid onto the wooden chassis. The Lid is a rectangular wooden panel with a white breadboard mounted on top.
The Action: An orange dashed arrow indicates that the Lid slides horizontally into long, narrow notches (grooves) running along the top inner edges of the side panels.
The Mechanical Constraint: A warning icon (an exclamation point in a circle) appears at the bottom left with the text: "Back piece needs to be open to slide the lid in and out." This indicates that the Back piece from Step 2 must be lifted or removed to clear the path for the Lid.
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "4" is displayed, and four out of five circles are filled in.
Text Prompt: "Slide Lid into notches."
Tactile Assembly Descriptions:
Tactile Tracks: A student can feel a long, recessed groove on the inside of each side panel. The wooden Lid is designed to "tongue-and-groove" into these tracks.
The Breadboard Landmark: The breadboard is a thick, white plastic rectangle with a grid of holes. It sits on top of the Lid and serves as a handle for sliding the assembly in and out.
The "Gate" (Back Piece): This is a critical logical step. Because the Back piece sits in a vertical keyhole, it acts as a "deadbolt." If the Back piece is pushed all the way down, it blocks the grooves. It must be slid "up" to allow the Lid to pass through.
Fit and Friction: The Lid should slide smoothly but snugly. If it feels stuck, check that the side panels are perfectly parallel and not tilted inward.
g.title: Step 4 Assembly Diagram

g.desc:
A two-part blueprint showing the assembly and mounting of the ultrasonic range finder.
Left Detail: Labeled "Use O-Ring to attach Range Finder to Holder." It shows a rectangular circuit board with two large circular sensors (the ultrasonic eyes) being joined to a uniquely shaped wooden "Holder" piece. A black O-ring (rubber band) loops around the back of the circuit board and hooks into small notches on the sides of the wooden holder.
Right Detail: Labeled "Attach to the front of your Base Station with Screws and Nuts." The completed holder-and-sensor assembly is shown mounting to the front lip of the chassis. Two screws pass upward through the bottom plate, securing the holder with hex nuts.
Progress Marker: In the top left corner, the number "5" is displayed, and all five circles are now filled in, indicating the final step.
Tactile Assembly Description:
The O-Ring Tension: Unlike the rigid screws, the O-ring provides a flexible, tension-based hold. A student can feel the rubber band stretching across the back of the sensor board and snapping into the side "hooks" of the wooden holder.
The "Eyes": The ultrasonic range finder is very distinct by touch—it has two hollow metal cylinders protruding from a flat circuit board. These should face forward, away from the robot.
The "Nose" Mount: The holder piece sits on a small shelf at the very front of the bottom plate. Feeling for the two screw holes on that front shelf will help align the sensor perfectly.
Final Stability: Once these screws are tightened, the robot's "head" is fixed in place. The chassis is now a complete, rigid structure ready for wiring.
g.title: Step 5 Assembly Diagram

g.desc:
The finished wooden chassis in a completed state from an isometric perspective. The white breadboard is securely mounted on the top deck, and the ultrasonic range finder "eyes" are visible at the front. The entire robot stands ready on its wooden legs. Below the image, the instruction reads: "Head to Piper Make to continue your mission."
Final Tactile & Functional Check:
Chassis Integrity: The robot should feel solid and not rattle when gently shaken. All six screws (three on each side) should be tight.
The "Lid" Slide: Verify that the lid can still slide in and out of its grooves when the back piece is lifted, and that the back piece "locks" it in place when pushed down.
Sensor Orientation: The two metallic cylinders of the ultrasonic sensor should be pointing straight ahead, unobstructed by any wooden parts.
The "Mission" Transition: The mention of Piper Make signals the move to the coding environment. The student will now need their Micro USB cable and USB adapter to connect this physical build to their computer.
g.title: Final Assembly
